speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really unwell lately—especially over the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in today. Let’s start by reviewing your recent evaluation—we confirmed a diagnosis of Coronary Artery Disease on June 29th.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature was elevated at 38.4°C during that visit, suggesting an acute inflammatory or infectious process.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I had a low-grade fever and chills—I thought it might be the flu, but it hasn’t gone away.

speaker: doctor
text: I see. Let’s check your vitals again now—and I’d like to review your recent lab work and symptoms more closely.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose was markedly elevated at 14.0 mmol/L on June 29th—well above the target range for someone with known CAD and diabetes risk.

speaker: patient
text: That’s right—I’ve been extremely fatigued, even after resting. It’s hard to get through the day without napping.

speaker: doctor
text: Fatigue plus hyperglycemia and fever raises concern for possible undiagnosed or decompensated diabetes—or an intercurrent illness stressing your system.

speaker: doctor
text: We started you on Lisinopril 20mg once daily on June 29th to support cardiac afterload reduction and renal protection, given your CAD and borderline glucose elevation.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin was 172 g/L—within normal limits for males, which is reassuring regarding oxygen-carrying capacity.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking my blood sugar at home since then. My readings have been all over the place—mostly high, especially in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Have you noticed any other symptoms—like increased thirst, frequent urination, or blurred vision?

speaker: patient
text: Yes—definitely more thirsty, and I’m up two or three times at night to urinate. My vision has been a little blurry too.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic signs of hyperglycemia. Given your CAD, persistent glucose >14 mmol/L, and new symptoms, we need to escalate your glycemic management urgently.

speaker: patient
text: I’m worried—could this be related to my heart condition worsening?

speaker: doctor
text: It’s interconnected. Uncontrolled glucose accelerates atherosclerosis and increases cardiac strain. We’ll address both together.

speaker: patient
text: I also had my fingerstick test this morning before breakfast—it was 14.0 again.

speaker: doctor
text: That reading matches the value from your initial workup—so this isn’t a one-off spike, but sustained hyperglycemia requiring intervention.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, that number—the one from last time—is what I got again just now.

speaker: doctor
text: Given this persistent elevation and your symptom progression, I am going to switch you to metformin 500mg twice daily starting today, in addition to continuing Lisinopril, and refer you to endocrinology for further optimization.
